## My first JavaScript loop

It was the night before my web dev class started. I opened Codecademy and tried writing a `for` loop that prints numbers 1 to 10. I kept getting `ReferenceError: i is not defined` because I forgot the `let` keyword.

- Spent 25 minutes staring at four lines of code
- Asked ChatGPT for help and learned about block scoping
- Finally saw the numbers print one by one in green console text

> _"The error wasn't punishing me, it was teaching me."_

I screenshotted the output and sent it to my best friend who already works at a startup. He replied with a single thumbs-up emoji that meant more than any compliment.

expected_leaves.content: The night before my web development class began, I opened Codecademy and attempted to write a `for` loop printing the integers from 1 to 10.
I spent about 25 minutes debugging a `ReferenceError: i is not defined` caused by omitting the `let` keyword in the loop initializer.
I realized that the error message was not a punishment but a teacher, which changed how I felt about debugging from that day forward.
After it worked, I screenshotted the green console output and sent it to my friend at a startup who replied with a single thumbs-up emoji.

expected_leaves.content: Mình viết hàm giai thừa đệ quy bằng Python và gặp `RecursionError` kéo dài suốt khoảng ba tiếng đồng hồ không tìm ra nguyên nhân.
Nguyên nhân thật sự là gõ nhầm `factorial(n+1)` thay vì `factorial(n-1)`, khiến đệ quy không bao giờ chạm tới base case.

## Học arrow function lúc 1 giờ sáng

Đọc tài liệu MDN về arrow function mà không hiểu nổi vì sao `this` lại khác. Mình thử viết `setTimeout(() => console.log(this.name), 1000)` trong một class và nó in ra đúng tên object — điều mà function thường không làm được.

- `function() {}` tạo `this` mới
- `() => {}` thừa kế `this` từ scope cha
- Cảm giác như vừa mở được một cánh cửa bí mật trong ngôn ngữ

Mình gửi note này vào nhóm chat lớp lúc 1 giờ sáng, không ai online, nhưng việc viết ra đã giúp mình nhớ mãi.

expected_leaves.content: Mình ngồi đọc tài liệu MDN về arrow function trong JavaScript lúc gần 1 giờ sáng và lúc đầu không hiểu tại sao `this` lại có hành vi khác với function thường.
Khi viết `setTimeout(() => console.log(this.name), 1000)` trong một class, arrow function in ra đúng tên object thay vì `undefined` như function thường.
Arrow function không tạo `this` riêng mà thừa kế `this` từ scope cha bao quanh nó, khác với function declaration tạo binding mới mỗi lần gọi.

metadata.meaning: thừa kế this từ scope cha thay vì tạo binding mới

expected_leaves.content: Việc viết ghi chú và gửi vào group chat lớp lúc 1 giờ sáng tuy không ai trả lời nhưng đã giúp mình ghi nhớ kiến thức lâu hơn nhiều so với chỉ đọc.

## Day I broke production for the first time

It was Thursday, 4:47 PM. I had been an intern for two weeks. I ran a migration on staging — or so I thought. The terminal looked identical to staging. Five minutes later, Slack lit up.

- Production users table had been truncated
- I had connected to prod URL by accident
- My manager said only one word in DM: _"Call me."_

> "I learned that day that no apology fixes a deleted table — only the backup does."

The restore took 90 minutes. I did not eat dinner. The next morning, my manager handed me a coffee and a printed checklist for connecting to databases.

expected_leaves.content: During my second week as an intern, on a Thursday at 4:47 PM, I ran a database migration believing I was connected to staging but I was actually connected to production.
The migration truncated the production users table and Slack notifications started arriving within five minutes from affected internal teams.
Restoring from backup took 90 minutes during which I skipped dinner and my manager messaged only the words "Call me." in direct message.
The lesson stayed with me forever: no apology can fix a deleted table — only the backup can, and the next morning I received a printed checklist for database connections.

## Day I learned Git rebase the hard way

I had been using Git for six months with only `add`, `commit`, `push`. My team lead suggested I rebase my feature branch onto main. I confidently typed `git rebase main` and ten conflict markers appeared at once.

- I panicked and ran `git rebase --abort` after the second file
- My lead pair-programmed with me for 40 minutes resolving them
- We resolved 7 conflicts using `git mergetool` and skipped 3 obsolete ones

> _"Rebase is a conversation between your past and present self."_

That night I added a sticky note: "Always `git status` between rebase steps."

expected_leaves.content: After six months using only `add`, `commit`, and `push`, I attempted my first `git rebase` onto main and immediately saw ten conflict markers across multiple files.
I panicked and ran `git rebase --abort` after the second file, then asked my team lead to pair-program through the rebase with me.
Together we used `git mergetool` to resolve seven conflicts and skipped three obsolete ones over a 40-minute session.
My takeaway was that rebase is a conversation between your past and present self, and I wrote a sticky note reminding myself to always run `git status` between rebase steps.

## My first hackathon, 48 hours straight

It was a college hackathon in February. My team of three chose to build a Chrome extension that translates highlighted text using DeepL. We had never used the Chrome extension API before.

- Hour 12: manifest.json finally loaded without errors
- Hour 26: API integration worked, but rate limit hit us
- Hour 41: presented a barely-working demo to the judges

> _"We did not win, but I learned more in 48 hours than in two semesters."_

We slept on the floor of the lecture hall. The pizza was cold. The bug was warm. The friendship was forged.

expected_leaves.content: In February my team of three competed in a college hackathon and chose to build a Chrome extension translating highlighted text using the DeepL API.
It took us until hour 12 to make manifest.json load without errors and until hour 26 to get the DeepL integration working before hitting a rate limit.
At hour 41 we presented a barely-working demo to the judges and slept on the lecture hall floor with cold pizza throughout the weekend.
Although we did not win, I felt I learned more about web APIs and team dynamics in those 48 hours than across two entire university semesters.

## Conquering my first leetcode hard

The problem was "Trapping Rain Water". I had been stuck on it for three weeks, attempting two-pointer, then dynamic programming, then monotonic stack.

- Attempt 1: brute force `O(n²)` — TLE on input size 20000
- Attempt 2: precompute left/right max arrays — `O(n)` time, accepted
- Attempt 3: two-pointer for `O(1)` extra space — most elegant

> _"Acceptance came at 11:47 PM on a rainy Tuesday — fitting."_

I took a screenshot of the green "Accepted" badge and pinned it on my desk. Three weeks of failures had earned me one moment of victory.

expected_leaves.content: I had been stuck on the leetcode hard problem "Trapping Rain Water" for three weeks, attempting two-pointer, dynamic programming, and monotonic stack approaches.
My brute force `O(n²)` attempt timed out on the input size 20000, but precomputing left and right max arrays gave a linear time accepted solution.
My third attempt used the two-pointer technique to achieve `O(1)` extra space, which I considered the most elegant of the three solutions.
The acceptance came at 11:47 PM on a rainy Tuesday, and I pinned the green "Accepted" screenshot on my desk as a reminder that persistence pays off.

## Day I gave up Java and never looked back

I spent six months trying to learn Java because everyone said it was "the corporate language". Spring Boot configuration files made me cry. XML schemas felt like ancient runes.

- Switched to Python over one weekend in March
- Built a working scraper on day 2
- Understood OOP better in Python than I ever did in Java

> _"The language did not betray me — I just was not its kind of person."_

I gave my Java textbook to my cousin, who happens to love verbose syntax. Two years later he interns at a bank. We are both happy.

expected_leaves.content: I spent six months trying to learn Java because peers insisted it was the corporate language, but Spring Boot configuration files and XML schemas overwhelmed me.
Over a single weekend in March I switched to Python and built a working web scraper by the second day of learning.

I gave my Java textbook to my cousin who loves verbose syntax — he later interned at a bank using Java, proof that languages fit different kinds of people.

## JLPT N3 — taken twice, passed once

First attempt: failed by 3 points on the listening section. Second attempt nine months later: passed with 117/180. The gap was not vocabulary — it was rhythm of conversation.

- Watched Terrace House without subtitles for 4 months
- Switched from Anki to shadowing JapanesePod101 episodes
- Started thinking out loud in Japanese while cooking

> _"Listening is the muscle you cannot build by reading."_

The result email arrived at midnight on a Wednesday. I shouted in the empty apartment, scaring the cat off the couch. The cat forgave me by morning.

expected_leaves.content: My first JLPT N3 attempt failed by 3 points on the listening section despite passing vocabulary and grammar comfortably.
Nine months later my second attempt passed with a score of 117 out of 180 after I changed strategy entirely toward listening practice.
My new approach combined watching Terrace House without subtitles for 4 months, shadowing JapanesePod101 episodes, and thinking aloud in Japanese while cooking.
The realization stayed with me that listening is a muscle which cannot be built by reading alone, no matter how many kanji or grammar points you memorize.

## Learning to swim at 28

I never learned to swim as a child. At 28, after a friend's pool party where I sat by the edge the whole afternoon, I signed up for adult beginner lessons at a community center.

- Lesson 5: a wobbly 10-meter freestyle
- Lesson 14: 100 meters non-stop, badly but completed

> _"I cried in the shower after lesson 14 — happy tears, not chlorine."_

Now I swim 1km twice a week. My instructor, a 60-year-old woman named Phương, says I am her favorite slow student.

## Math olympiad failure that changed everything

The national math olympiad, my senior year of high school. I solved problem 1 in 15 minutes, then spent 4 hours on problem 2 without progress. The other three problems I never even read.

- Total score: 7/40
- Ranking: bottom third
- Coach said: "You optimized for depth when you needed breadth"

> _"That sentence is now taped above my desk a decade later."_

I never won a math medal, but the lesson on breadth-versus-depth applies to every project I lead now: scan the surface first, then dive only where it matters.

- Conclusion: "Too modest about your own contribution."

> _"I went home and slept for 12 hours straight."_

The next morning I revised every slide. The real defense felt remarkably calm. Both senior PhDs attended and one whispered after, "You listened." That word — listened — was worth the brutal mock.

- Lặn 15m: thiếu 2m, ngoi lên thở sớm

> "Thầy gọi tên 5 bạn vào đội — có tên mình."

Thầy nói mình kỹ thuật còn yếu nhưng tinh thần không bỏ cuộc. 4 năm trong đội tuyển dạy mình rằng kỷ luật buổi sáng quan trọng hơn tài năng bẩm sinh.

expected_leaves.content: Năm lớp 8 mình đăng ký thử việc đội tuyển bơi của trường vì thầy thể dục cho rằng mình có thể hình phù hợp, vòng tuyển gồm 3 nội dung kiểm tra.
Kết quả ba nội dung: 50m tự do mình về thứ 4 trên 12 thí sinh, đạp nước 60 giây vừa đủ thời gian, lặn 15m thiếu 2m phải ngoi lên thở sớm.
Thầy chọn 5 bạn vào đội và có tên mình, dù thầy nhận xét kỹ thuật còn yếu nhưng tinh thần không bỏ cuộc là điểm cộng quyết định.
Bốn năm trong đội tuyển bơi dạy mình rằng kỷ luật buổi sáng dậy sớm tập luyện quan trọng hơn nhiều so với tài năng bẩm sinh.

note:
## Phỏng vấn vào trường nội trú cấp ba

Lớp 9, mình thi vào trường THPT chuyên Hà Nội — Amsterdam. Sau bài viết và phỏng vấn nhóm, mình vào vòng phỏng vấn 1-1 với giáo viên Văn.

- Câu 1: "Em vừa đọc cuốn sách nào gần đây?"
- Câu 2: "Em đồng tình với nhân vật nào nhất, vì sao?"
- Câu 3: "Nếu được viết lại đoạn kết, em sẽ viết thế nào?"

> _"Mình nói say sưa về Số đỏ của Vũ Trọng Phụng suốt 8 phút."_

Hai tuần sau giấy báo trúng tuyển đến nhà. Mẹ khóc trước cả mình. Ba năm cấp ba ở Ams sau đó định hình toàn bộ con người mình hiện tại.

expected_leaves.content: Năm lớp 9 mình thi vào trường THPT chuyên Hà Nội Amsterdam, qua bài viết và phỏng vấn nhóm rồi vào vòng phỏng vấn 1-1 với giáo viên Văn.
Ba câu hỏi phỏng vấn xoay quanh sách: hỏi cuốn vừa đọc gần đây, nhân vật đồng tình nhất và lý do, cách mình sẽ viết lại đoạn kết nếu được chọn.
Mình nói say sưa về cuốn "Số đỏ" của Vũ Trọng Phụng suốt 8 phút, phân tích nhân vật Xuân Tóc Đỏ như một sản phẩm xã hội thay vì kẻ xấu cá nhân.
Hai tuần sau giấy báo trúng tuyển đến nhà, mẹ khóc trước cả mình, và ba năm cấp ba ở Amsterdam sau đó định hình toàn bộ con người mình hiện tại.
